data.title: The Memo: US allies grapple with a world order upended by Trump
data.description:
A tumultuous period between the United States and its traditional allies took another lurch Friday, as NATO nations expressed outrage over remarks from President Trump the previous day.

The new furor began when Trump downplayed the willingness of the U.S.’s partners to sacrifice on behalf of the alliance.

“We have never really asked anything of them. You know, they’ll say they sent some troops to Afghanistan, or this or that. And they did — they stayed a little back, a little off the front lines,” Trump said in an interview with Maria Bartiromo of Fox Business.

In fact, many hundreds of non-American NATO troops lost their lives in Afghanistan during the 20-year war there. The fatalities included more than 450 British troops and more than 150 Canadian troops.

The anger about Trump’s remarks in those nations was immediate and visceral.

United Kingdom Prime Minister Keir Starmer said the comments were “insulting and frankly appalling.”

The fresh controversy fuels an even bigger story. Nations typically aligned with the U.S. are grappling with Trump’s propensity to upend the established order.

Trump’s recent suggestion that he could use military force, or punitive tariffs, to seize control of Greenland stunned NATO members — and their shock didn’t disappear merely because he backed off in the end.

The Greenland issue came on top of European anxiety over Trump’s approach to the war in Ukraine; tensions around a new U.S. National Security Strategy that lambasted Europe as suffering from a lack of “civilizational self-confidence”; and a more general trepidation about the president’s belligerence and volatility.

The White House defended the NATO troop remarks, with multiple media outlets reporting a statement from deputy press secretary Anna Kelly:

“President Trump is right – America’s contributions to NATO dwarf that of other countries, and his success in delivering a five percent spending pledge from NATO allies is helping Europe take greater responsibility for its own defense,” she said.

Kelly also defended Trump’s actions on Greenland, saying that the U.S. was “the only NATO partner” that could defend the territory and that he is “advancing NATO interests in doing so.”

Other U.S. voices take a diametrically different view, recoiling at the president’s remarks.

“It’s awful; it’s a travesty; it’s ignorant. It’s so wrong,” Jim Townsend, a former deputy assistant secretary of Defense who is now affiliated with the Center for a New American Security, told this column.

Citing his long experience at the Pentagon, Townsend said of non-American NATO allies, “I know what they did. He is absolutely wrong. They bled like we did.”

From the United Kingdom, Alastair Campbell, who served as then-Prime Minister Tony Blair’s director of communications in the years before and after 9/11, blasted Trump’s remarks as “utterly disgusting.”

“It’s so casually dismissive of all non-Americans: ‘Yeah, yeah, they sent us a few troops’. It’s that ‘couldn’t-give-a-s‑‑‑’ way he speaks about people,” Campbell told this column.

Campbell, who remains well-connected within the governing Labour Party and is also the co-host of one of the U.K.’s most popular podcasts, insists that the rest of the world needs to stand up to Trump’s political threats with far more vigor.

“It’s true prime ministers cannot speak in the way that I would,” he said. “But I think Trump will get worse and worse and worse unless people make him understand that no matter how powerful America is, it can’t run the world alone.”

The problem for Europeans and other allies is that it’s hard to see exactly what a world looks like in which the U.S. has abandoned its traditional role.

In Davos, Switzerland, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney’s speech received enormous attention, in part because of its key line: “We are in the midst of a rupture, not a transition.”

Carney outlined what that meant to him. Nations like Canada could try “building what we claim to believe in, rather than waiting for the old order to be restored,” he said.

In more concrete terms, he proposed “different coalitions for different issues based on common values and interests.”

He cited Canada’s membership of the Coalition of the Willing to defend Ukraine and its “standing firmly” with Denmark and Greenland against Trump’s imperial ambitions.

Even so, there is an awareness of just how difficult it is for other nations to create a whole new geopolitical landscape — regardless of how alienated they feel by Trump’s rhetoric and bellicosity.

Nobody thinks Washington can simply be ignored.

Kyle Harrietha, an Ottawa-based public affairs consultant who was previously chief of staff to Canadian Energy Minister Jonathan Wilkinson, noted that Trump talked about making Canada the “51st state” of the U.S. soon after being sworn in.

“Now you are seeing the rest of the world — Europe in particular — coming to grips with this in a way that Canadians were ahead of the curve on,” Harrietha said.

But he also acknowledged, “Ultimately, our geography dictates that we have to engage with the United States. … We are still allies, our militaries still work together, and Canada has responded to some of the president’s demands — with military spending, which many U.S. presidents have raised; Arctic security; and fentanyl issues, though that was not necessarily a major issue in Canada.”

Campbell, from Britain, argued for a somewhat similar approach that, broadly speaking, resists Trump but still ensures “the relationship is working at every other level — military, intelligence, business, trade.”

Campbell emphasized that domestic political concerns are part of the picture too.

He said he understood the view taken by Starmer even when tensions over Greenland were at their apex.

“Keir Starmer would argue one of the reasons he got [Trump] to climb down is because he doesn’t go out and do the public grandstanding,” Campbell said.

“But the point I feel, as a Labour person, is that every time that happens, Keir drains his political authority at home. If he doesn’t come out, people will think, ‘you cannot just allow this guy to intimidate you.’”

On the issue of British sacrifices for NATO, Starmer plainly did feel he had to speak out. But Campbell’s bigger point remains salient.

A YouGov tracking poll in December tested Trump’s favorability ratings in six western European nations. The president was viewed unfavorably by more than 70 percent of adults — and favorably by fewer than 20 percent — in each one of those nations.

Faced with the daunting prospect of trying to assemble a whole new web of alliances, some in the U.S. and beyond are simply trying to get through to an era beyond Trump.

Daniel Fried, who served as U.S. ambassador to Poland during the Clinton presidency and is now a fellow at the Atlantic Council, told this column that he didn’t think the last tumultuous period would “permanently damage the trans-Atlantic alliance. But it will permanently damage Trump’s ability to deal with the Europeans.”

“The trans-Atlantic alliance can recover, especially in a post-Trump era,” Fried added.

For all the discussions this week, the European mood might have been most succinctly captured by Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen when she visited Starmer at Chequers, the British prime minister’s country estate, on Thursday.

Signing the guest book, Frederiksen alluded to the Beatles — and to the uneasy state of the world.

“We’ll get by with a little help from our friends,” she wrote.

The Memo is a reported column by Niall Stanage.

data.title: Crockett, Talarico to face off in first Texas Senate primary debate
data.description:
Texas Democrats Rep. Jasmine Crockett and state Rep. James Talarico are set for a Saturday showdown in the first debate of their closely watched Senate primary. 

The stakes are high for both campaigns, as new polling shows Talarico with a single-digit lead over Crockett, whose entry into the race last month added a new layer to the party’s long-shot hopes of winning in the red state.

Just weeks before squaring off in a March primary, the rivals will have to make their case for statewide electability on the debate stage as they race to replace incumbent Sen. John Cornyn (R), who faces his own tough challenge in the GOP primary.

The debate, hosted by the Texas AFL-CIO, will serve as an early test of whether either Crockett or Talarico can “capture lightning in a bottle this year,” said Jon Taylor, the University of Texas at San Antonio’s department chair of political science.

“Either Talarico or Crockett needs to be emphasizing this: that they know that they’re in trouble, folks, and … [saying] ‘I’m the person to get us over the top and get a victory in the state, to strike a blow against Trump, strike a blow against MAGA,’” Taylor said. 

Democrats have struggled for decades to win statewide, after the last Democratic senator was ousted in the early 1990s. Cook Political Report rates the Senate race as “likely Republican,” just a step down from being solidly in the GOP column.

But the party is nonetheless bullish about making inroads in the red state. 

Former Rep. Beto O’Rourke (D-Texas) came close to toppling Sen. Ted Cruz (R) amid 2018’s “blue wave,” and former Rep. Colin Allred (D-Texas) lost a competitive race against Cruz in 2024. Now, the party sees Cornyn’s Senate seat as integral to their uphill climb to flip the Senate this fall. 

Both Talarico and Crockett have excited political operatives. But their campaigns, which are ideologically similar, have sparked debate about what tactics are best to win a tough general election in Texas. 

“I think that a lot of people are being pragmatic about this race, and they want to know how you’re going to win in November,” said Matt Angle, a Democratic strategist who founded the Lone Star Project, an organization that works to get Democrats elected in the state. 

“I think that better than trying to point fingers and do any ‘gotcha’ moments, I think that it will be the person who best demonstrates how they can break through and win in Texas, because the stakes are so high,” Angle said of what either candidate should bring to the debate stage. 

Talarico, a Presbyterian seminarian and former schoolteacher, saw his star rise quickly last year as one of the Democrats who fled Texas to stall a GOP-led redistricting plan. He’s leveraged social media to advance his profile, sharing his work in the state Legislature or sermons at church. 

He’s raised more than $13 million since launching in September, a sign of strength despite his comparatively lower name recognition. AdImpact data also shows Talarico and groups supporting him have significantly outspent the Crockett camp by a roughly 10-to-1 ratio so far, and roughly 5-to-1 since Crockett joined the race. 

In a recent Emerson College Polling/Nexstar Media survey, Talarico had a 9-point edge over Crockett among likely Democratic primary voters, though 15 percent were still undecided. 

But many operatives in the state were skeptical about the polling at this point in the race, despite seeing Talarico as a strong contender. Taylor said he’s heard from Texans who are “definitely intent” on voting in the primary but still like both candidates and “have no idea” who they’ll support in November.

“I don’t think there’s a clear winner or clear leader to find right now,” Angle said of the primary. “And I think the race has really come down to a battle of styles.”

Crockett, a progressive leader on Capitol Hill and a former public defender, has yet to release fundraising figures for the few weeks since her last-minute decision to run scrambled the political calculus for Texas Democrats. 

In a Crockett-sponsored HIT Strategies survey that contrasts the Emerson results, the House member fared 13 points better than Talarico, the El Paso Times reported this week, with another 21 percent undecided.

A December poll from Texas Southern University had also shown Crockett leading by 8 points. 

Allred, who came within 9 points of the upper chamber against Cruz in 2024, dropped a 2026 Senate bid to run for a House seat amid chatter that Crockett would jump in, teeing up a tense primary with Talarico. 

Known in the House for her viral sparring with Republicans, Crockett has offered up an aggressive anti-Trump approach that proponents say could give the party a fighting chance for the Senate seat — especially if Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R), a close Trump ally who has a controversial background in the state, defeats Cornyn for the GOP nomination. 

Paxton made headlines last week with an AI-generated attack ad depicting Cornyn dancing with Crockett, a move that Crockett suggested to The New York Times is a signal of her strength. 

But other observers argue that, in a state that went to Trump by double digits in 2024, Crockett’s posture could be a gift to the Republican nominee. 

“It really just comes down to: Do we want fire and brimstone? Or do we want a more measured response from our senator?” Texas Democratic strategist Joel Montfort said, predicting the Talarico-Crockett debate will be “a battle of personalities.” 

Montfort also forecast that the Democratic rivals — though they’ve been in a cordial competition so far — may be forced to shift to a more combative posture as the primary approaches.

“Because you can only have one winner. And how do you differentiate yourself? Go after the other candidate. I don’t imagine this being cordial all the way to the end, especially now that … [the] clock’s running,” he said. 

Early voting begins Feb. 17 for the March 3 primary, giving candidates just a few weeks to make their case to the primary electorate while laying the groundwork for an uphill general election fight.

Regardless of which progressive name emerges, strategists forecast that Democrats’ chances will hinge heavily on the results of the Republican primary. 

The recent Emerson poll found Paxton with 27 percent to Cornyn’s 26 percent. Another 16 percent supported Rep. Wesley Hunt (R-Texas), preventing any candidate from snagging a majority.

If no candidate wins more than half the votes on the first go, the three-way fight could push the GOP primary into a May runoff. 

In hypothetical general election match-ups, the Emerson poll found Paxton tied with both Talarico and Crockett — while Cornyn defeated Talarico by 3 points and Crockett by 5 points. 

Montfort forecast that either Paxton or Cornyn are “going to have to fight for every single point they get” in November’s general election. 

In light of lackluster approval numbers for Trump and Republicans in Texas, Democrats are drawing parallels to the 2018 midterms, hopeful that either Talarico or Crockett can harness the energy that brought O’Rourke two points away from ousting Cruz that cycle.

O’Rourke, though, told the Texas Take podcast last week he won’t be weighing in on the primary battle, calling both Democrats “generational talents” who can win in the fall. 

“The sky’s the limit on what we can do here,” Montfort said.

__

The hourlong Saturday debate, moderated by KXAN-TV anchor Daniel Marin and Dallas Morning News political writer Gromer Jeffers, will air on Nexstar Texas stations at 2 p.m. CST. 

“We’re looking forward to hosting the first U.S. Senate debate this year focused on the issues that matter to Texas workers,” Texas AFL-CIO President Leonard Aguilar said in a release. “This debate will help our union members — and workers across the state — determine who to support in the Democratic primary.”

data.title: Supreme Court draws who's who of spectators during high-stakes term
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and Fed board of governors member Lisa Cook faced a choice ahead of this week’s Supreme Court oral argument: show up, or sit it out? 

They chose visibility, forming part of a crowd of economic and legal heavyweights in the justices’ lines of sight as they mulled the future of the Fed’s protections from the executive branch. 

Some others involved in recent high-profile Supreme Court cases have stayed away for their own strategic reasons.  

But on Wednesday, Cook and a crowd of supporters became the latest to show up, hoping to persuade the justices that ruling for President Trump in her lawsuit would effectively end the Fed’s longstanding independence to run the nation’s monetary policy. 

It was a physical display of the institutional stakes. Trump became the first president to try to fire a sitting Fed governor in its 113-year history when he fired Cook in August over mortgage fraud accusations, which she denies. 

Cook watched the arguments from the center section of the public gallery, which spans the back half of the grandiose, column-lined room. She sat among a line of others who chatted as they waited for the justices to take the bench. 

Rep. Joyce Beatty (Ohio) was one of several Democratic lawmakers who made it inside. They were part of a rally outside opposing Trump.

On the other side of the center section was Ben Bernanke, who led the Fed through the 2008 financial crisis. Now more than a decade removed from the role, he and other former top economic officials are backing Cook. 

Bernanke sat with a larger group, most of whom scribbled notes throughout the two-hour session. Bernanke, however, appeared to doze off at points. By the time Justice Clarence Thomas had asked the first question, Bernanke’s eyes were closed, chin against his chest. 

Inside, Powell wasn’t near any of the others. The Fed chair sat on the far-right side of the courtroom with his wife, nearly against the wall and close to the seating for Supreme Court clerks and staff. 

He looked ahead toward the bench for most of the argument, his chin slightly raised, appearing to listen intently. Afterward, he quickly left the building through a side door, away from the swarm of cameras. 

Powell plays a unique role in the case. 

He has increasingly pushed back vocally on Trump’s attempts to influence the Fed and the Justice Department’s criminal investigation into him over his congressional testimony concerning the overbudget renovations at the Fed’s headquarters. 

In Cook’s case, Powell is a named a defendant alongside the Federal Reserve itself and Trump, meaning that Solicitor General D. John Sauer at the argument was technically representing Powell, not just the president. 

Powell didn’t take Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent’s advice not to go. 

“I actually think that’s a mistake,” Bessent told CNBC Tuesday while in Davos, Switzerland, after reporting surfaced of Powell’s expected attendance. 

“Because if you’re trying not to politicize the Fed, for the Fed chair to be sitting there trying to put his thumb on the scale is a real mistake,” Bessent continued. 

Bessent himself attended Supreme Court oral arguments in November, when the justices considered the legality of Trump’s sweeping tariffs. Several other administration officials came as well. 

As Bessent left the courtroom that day, he gave The Hill a thumbs up when asked how the argument went. 

Even Trump himself had floated attending. 

“I have an obligation to go there,” he told reporters in the Oval Office in October. 

It wasn’t the first time Trump toyed with going to the Supreme Court. As he campaigned for his second term, he wanted to see oral arguments on his assertion of broad criminal immunity as a former president. But the New York judge overseeing Trump’s then-ongoing criminal trial rejected his request, ordering Trump to stay in Manhattan. 

When the tariffs argument at the court came around, Trump ultimately passed on going, writing on social media, “I do not want to distract from the importance of this Decision.” 

The next month, the Supreme Court moved to its next major battle involving Trump: his firing of Federal Trade Commission member Rebecca Slaughter.  

Slaughter sat in the gallery with two other agency leaders sacked by Trump: National Labor Relations Board member Gwynne Wilcox and U.S. Copyright Office head Shira Perlmutter. Their lawsuits could be impacted by the justices’ decision. 

Avoiding Supreme Court oral arguments can also reveal a strategy. 

At last week’s sessions on the constitutionality of transgender athlete bans, one of the two student athletes suing attended. The other did not. 

Both decisions came with little surprise. 

Becky Pepper-Jackson, the 15-year-old discus thrower and transgender girl challenging West Virginia’s ban, sat in. Her lawyers have stressed West Virginia’s law is all about their client.  

In the first paragraph of their opening brief, they note she is the only person whom West Virginia’s law has been known to apply. And Pepper-Jackson long ago shoved aside the protections that enable minors to maintain privacy when they sue, making herself available to the press and posing for photos in front of the high court. 

Lindsay Hecox, the other athlete suing, who is challenging Idaho’s ban, took the opposite approach.  

She steered clear of the courtroom and did not conduct a press tour. The American Civil Liberties Union represents both students, but the post-argument press release only contained a statement from Pepper-Jackson. Not Hecox. 

Hecox has been attempting to drop her lawsuit. She says negative public attention has caused her to give up trying to play college athletics, so the Supreme Court shouldn’t issue an opinion, but Idaho accuses her of trying to manipulate the court’s docket. 

In the courtroom last week, Justice Sonia Sotomayor appeared the most sympathetic to Hecox. The liberal justice scoffed at Idaho’s lawyer for disagreeing that being a plaintiff in a blockbuster Supreme Court case “makes your infamy live forever.” 

“Have you studied your law cases? Students do all the time,” Sotomayor bristled. 

For others, attending their Supreme Court oral arguments is a no-brainer. 

Victor Schwartz, the CEO of VOS Selections, the wine importer leading the fight against Trump’s tariffs, always planned to go to November’s arguments. 

“I don’t get opportunities to appear at the Supreme Court as a plaintiff too often,” Schwartz told The Hill at the time. 

In fact, he would’ve gotten more tickets if he could. But Schwartz only got one. It left his daughter spending hours waiting in the public line to try to get a coveted seat. 

“For us, it’s amazing,” Schwartz said. “We toil away in our little importing business. And next thing you know, we’re at the Supreme Court in Washington, suing the administration.  

“That’s kind of momentous.”

data.title: Interactive map: How much snow will fall in my area during the storm?
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – With dangerously-cold arctic air already settling over a large swath of the U.S. Friday, at least 177 million people are under watches or warnings for ice and snow.

States in the Northeast — no stranger to snowfall — are preparing for some of the region’s heaviest snow in years, with Connecticut Gov. Ned Lamont (D) urging people to stock up on food early and “stay home on Sunday.”

Forecasters say what makes this storm especially dangerous is how far south the cold is expected to creep, mixing with a storm from the Gulf that is projected to bring snow, sleet and freezing rain to southern states.

For residents wondering how much snow they can expect as hazardous winter storm travels east across the U.S., click or tap on the interactive map below showing projections for the coming 72 hours:

More than 1,000 flights have been delayed or canceled Friday, according to the flight tracking website FlightAware, with roughly 2,300 more on Saturday.

Schools were shuttered amid plummeting temperatures in Midwestern cities like Chicago and Cleveland, among others.

Utility companies in much of the eastern two-thirds of the U.S. are bracing for potential widespread outages from freezing rain, which can weigh down power lines and cause them to snap.

In at least 11 Southern states from Texas to Virginia, a majority of homes are heated by electricity, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Forecasters warned that the damage, especially in areas pounded by ice, could rival a hurricane.

For information on preparing for severe winter weather, see Ready.gov.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Cruz returns to Texas ahead of winter storm after travel backlash
data.description:
Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) said Friday he is back in the Lone Star State after a viral photo showed him on a plane as the state prepared for a massive winter storm.

“I’ve returned home from my work trip. It’s 66 degrees & beautiful. A storm is expected tomorrow night,” Cruz posted on the social platform X alongside a picture of himself standing outdoors.

“But I am reliably informed by Twitter that if I simply raise up my hand on Texas soil, the storm will turn around & sunshine, rainbows & unicorns will emerge. Let it be,” he added, jokingly.  

The senator’s post was spurred by a photo posted on Jan. 20 showing Cruz on a plane reportedly heading to Laguna Beach, Calif. The accompanying post has now garnered more than 9 million views.

In a statement to KXAN, Cruz’s office said the senator was on “pre-planned work travel” that had been scheduled for weeks in advance.

Mixed reactions poured in on social media, with some users asking, “Who cares?” and others mocking him for taking another ill-timed trip, referencing his travel to Cancun in 2021 as another deadly winter storm bore down on Texas.

“Texans know that when Ted Cruz is headed to a warm location (Laguna Beach), the freeze is going to be bad,” one user wrote.

“Ted Cruz on a plane is an omen of severe winter storms to come, similar to the waffle house index that gauges storm severity,” another user chimed in.

Cruz faced intense backlash for leaving his home state during the 2021 storm, which knocked out power for millions of Texans for several days.

He initially defended the travel by explaining that he was accompanying his daughters and would return the next day. But he later apologized, saying it was “obviously a mistake.”  

The National Weather Service has issued a winter storm warning for much of Texas due to the threat of frigid temperatures, freezing rain and sleet throughout the weekend; however, any impacts are not expected to reach the severity of 2021.

data.title: FBI agent who sought to investigate ICE officer in Minneapolis shooting resigns: Report
data.description:
An FBI agent in Minneapolis who attempted to probe the death of a 37-year-old woman killed by a federal immigration officer earlier this month has resigned, according to multiple reports.

Tracee Mergen left her job in the bureau’s Minneapolis field office after facing pressure to discontinue an inquiry into the Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer, Jonathan Ross, according to The New York Times, which first reported on Mergen’s resignation.

“It is FBI policy not to comment on personnel matters,” the FBI said in a statement to The Hill.

Mergen’s exit comes after the Justice Department (DOJ) said it sees “no basis” for a civil rights investigation into the fatal shooting of Renee Good.

Good was fatally shot while in her SUV on Jan. 7 during an immigration operation in a Minneapolis neighborhood. A private autopsy revealed Good had gunshot wounds to her left forearm, right breast and head, and that a fourth bullet grazed her body.

Senior Trump administration officials were quick to justify the shooting as an act of self-defense, claiming that Good had attempted to use her vehicle as a weapon in an act of domestic terrorism.

Democrats have widely disputed that narrative, accusing the administration of lying and calling for an independent investigation.

Federal investigators have refused to cooperate with local authorities, restricting their access to materials and evidence in the case after initially indicating it would be a joint investigation. That decision prompted Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension (BCA) Superintendent Drew Evans to issue a statement on Jan. 8 saying that his agency had “reluctantly withdrawn” from the investigation.

Less than a week later, the Times reported that six federal prosecutors in Minnesota had resigned over the DOJ’s push to investigate Good’s widow.

Federal officials have doubled down on their defense of Ross, who is now “in hiding,” according to White House border czar Tom Homan.

Vice President JD Vance suggested after the shooting that Ross was protected by “absolute immunity.” However, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem confirmed Sunday that DHS was conducting an internal review of his actions, saying he was placed on three days of administrative leave following the incident, per protocol.  

Meanwhile, the DOJ has subpoenaed Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D), who have been openly critical of the growing presence of federal agents in the state, for allegedly obstructing an immigration enforcement investigation.

Walz dismissed the subpoenas as a “partisan distraction,” and Frey accused the Trump administration of weaponizing its authority to “try to intimidate local leaders doing their jobs.”

Updated at 10:05 p.m. EST

data.title: Thousands rally against immigration enforcement in subzero Minnesota temperatures
data.description:
MINNEAPOLIS (AP) — Police arrested about 100 clergy demonstrating against immigration enforcement at Minnesota’s largest airport Friday, and several thousand gathered in downtown Minneapolis despite Arctic temperatures to protest the Trump administration’s crackdown.

The protests are part of a broader movement against President Trump’s increased immigration enforcement across the state, with labor unions, progressive organizations and clergy urging Minnesotans to stay away from work, school and even shops. The faith leaders gathered at the airport to protest deportation flights and urge airlines to call for an end to to what the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) has called its largest-ever immigration enforcement operation.

The clergy were issued misdemeanor citations of trespassing and failure to comply with a peace officer and were then released, said Jeff Lea, a Metropolitan Airports Commission spokesman. They were arrested outside the main terminal at the Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport because they went beyond the reach of their permit for demonstrating and disrupted airline operations, he said.

Rev. Mariah Furness Tollgaard of Hamline Church in St. Paul said police ordered them to leave but she and others decided to stay and be arrested to show support for migrants, including members of her congregation who are afraid to leave their homes. She planned to go back to her church after her brief detention to hold a prayer vigil.

“We cannot abide living under this federal occupation of Minnesota,” Tollgaard said.

Protesters demand ICE leave Minnesota

The Rev. Elizabeth Barish Browne traveled from Cheyenne, Wyoming, to participate in the rally in downtown Minneapolis, where the high temperature was minus 9 degrees Fahrenheit (minus 23 degrees Celsius) despite a bright sun.

“What’s happening here is clearly immoral,” the Unitarian Universalist minister said. “It’s definitely chilly, but the kind of ice that’s dangerous to us is not the weather.”

Protesters have gathered daily in the Twin Cities since Jan. 7, when 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good was fatally shot by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer. Federal law enforcement officers have repeatedly squared off with community members and activists who track their movements.

Sam Nelson said he skipped work so he could join the march. He said he’s a former student of the Minneapolis high school where federal agents detained someone after class earlier this month. That arrest led to altercations between federal officers and bystanders.

“It’s my community,” Nelson said. “Like everyone else, I don’t want ICE on our streets.”

Organizers said Friday morning that more than 700 businesses statewide have closed in solidarity with the movement, from a bookstore in tiny Grand Marais near the Canadian border to the landmark Guthrie Theater in downtown Minneapolis.

“We’re achieving something historic,” said Kate Havelin of Indivisible Twin Cities, one of the more than 100 participating groups.

FBI agent resigns over Good investigation

An FBI supervisory agent in Minnesota has resigned over the Justice Department’s handling of the investigation into Good’s killing, two people familiar with the matter said on Friday. The agent resigned because she felt pressured to not investigate the shooting in a way she felt the FBI would have ordinarily done, said the people, who spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not authorized to discuss personnel moves.

The FBI did not immediately respond to a message seeking comment.

The Hennepin County Medical Examiner, meanwhile, posted Good’s initial autopsy report online, which classified her death as a homicide and determined she died from “multiple gunshots wounds.”

A more detailed independent autopsy commissioned by Good’s family said one bullet pierced the left side her head and exited on the right side. This autopsy, released Wednesday through the Romanucci & Blandin law firm, said bullets also struck her in the arm and breast, although those injuries weren’t immediately life-threatening.

Detention of a 2-year-old and a 5-year-old

A 2-year-old was reunited with her mother Friday, a day after she was detained with her father outside of their home in South Minneapolis, lawyer Irina Vaynerman told The Associated Press.

Vaynerman said they had quickly challenged the family’s detention in federal court. The petition states that the child, a citizen of Ecuador, was brought to the U.S. as a newborn. The child and her father, Elvis Tipan Echeverria, both have a pending asylum application and neither are subject to final orders of removal.

A U.S. district judge on Thursday had barred the government from transferring the toddler out of state, but she and her father were on a commercial flight to Texas about 20 minutes later, according to court filings. They were flown back Friday.

Agents arrested Tipan Echeverria during a “targeted enforcement operation,” according to a DHS statement. DHS said the child’s mother was in the area but refused to take the child.

Vaynerman rejected that explanation, saying Tipan Echeverria was “not allowed” to bring his 2-year-old to her mother inside their home.

DHS repeated its allegation Friday that the father of 5-year-old Liam Ramos abandoned him during his arrest by immigration officers in Columbia Heights on Tuesday, leading to the child being detained, too.

Department spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin said Liam was detained because his father, Adrian Alexander Conejo Arias, “fled from the scene.” The two are detained together at the Dilley Detention Center in Texas, which is intended to hold families. McLaughlin said officers tried to get Liam’s mother to take him, but she refused to accept custody.

The family’s attorney Marc Prokosch said he thinks the mother refused to open the door to the ICE officers because she was afraid she would be detained. Columbia Heights district superintendent Zena Stenvik said Liam was “used as bait.”

Prokosch found nothing in state records to suggest Liam’s father has a criminal history.

On Friday, Border Patrol commander Gregory Bovino sought to shift the narrative away from Liam’s detention by attacking the news media for, in his view, insufficient coverage of children who have lost parents to violence by people in the country illegally. After briefly mentioning the 5-year-old during a news conference, he talked about a mother of five who was killed in August 2023.

data.title: Power out? Here are 5 ways to stay warm, and 1 you should never try
data.description:
MEMPHIS, Tenn. (WREG) — When winter weather hits, power outages are always a possibility, and we want to make sure you’re taking safety precautions, while staying warm.

In a matter of hours, much of the U.S. will experience its first winter storm of 2026, which means there’s a potential for power outages, amid the frigid temps, ice and snow.

Electric homes face the most challenges during power outages in frigid temperatures, but there are some ways you can stay warm, without a heater, says the Mississippi Emergency Management Agency (MEMA).

When the power goes out, electrical heaters are typically not an option for tenants or homeowners, but that’s not the only way you can stay warm.

“You can actually put up a tent inside a living room,” said MEMA’s Scott Simmons.

“Think about it: So you’re in a living room that has no power. You and a couple of people in the tent, hanging out, your carbon dioxide is captured inside. [It] warms the tent and you can stay warm.”

Simmons says wearing layers of clothing can also help keep you warm amid a power outage.

Ovens and stove tops should never be used as heating elements, but there are safer alternatives you can take, says Simmons.

“Take a couple of bricks, right? Put it on a pan or a tray. Light some candles inside and you got a little man-made warmer. The heat from the candles warms the bricks, the bricks radiate some heat,” he said.

Simmons says this man-made warmer won’t necessarily heat your entire home like a fireplace, but it will provide some warmth.

Fireplaces can be used during power outages, but he says there should have been a proper inspection beforehand, and you should collect wood now, before the winter weather arrives.

Simmons also wants people to know about the dangerous and deadly risks of using generators as a heating source during a power outage.

“The dangerous elements of a generator is the exhaust, alright? And I hate to say it, but some people have actually run a generator inside a house. The exhaust from a generator will kill you.”

He says placing a generator outside of your door can also cause problems, so it’s best you position it far away from your door, to prevent the exhaust from entering your home.

If you decide to sit inside your car to warm up, MEMA says cars can have exhaust issues too, so you shouldn’t run your car with the garage closed. Instead, you’re advised to keep it open or move your car outside of your garage or car port.

data.title: James Cameron: US 'a place where everybody's at each other's throats'
data.description:
Oscar-winning director James Cameron was candid during a recent interview about why he chose to leave the U.S. during the COVID-19 pandemic and permanently relocate to his 12,000-acre New Zealand farm, saying he wanted to live in a place that “believes in science.”

“Five or six months after the pandemic hit … [New Zealand] had eliminated the virus completely. They actually eliminated the virus twice. The third time when it showed up in a mutated form, it broke through,” Cameron told host Graham Bensinger on his podcast “In Depth with Graham Bensinger.”

“This is why I love New Zealand,” he continued, pointing to the country’s high vaccination rate. “People there are, for the most part, sane, as opposed to the United States, where you have a 62 percent vaccination rate, and that’s going down, going the wrong direction.”

Cameron then presented a choice, asking rhetorically, “Where would you rather live?”  

“Oh, right, a place that actually believes in science and is sane and where people can work together cohesively toward a common goal, or a place where everybody’s at each other’s throats, extremely polarized, turning its back on science and basically would be in utter disarray if another pandemic appears,” he said.

When Bensinger responded that the U.S. “is a fantastic place to live, but New Zealand is stunningly beautiful,” Cameron quipped: “I’m not there for the scenery, I’m there for the sanity.”

Cameron has been openly critical of President Trump for denying climate change and has expressed disapproval of the administration’s environmental policies.

The “Avatar” director likened Trump’s 2024 reelection to “watching a car crash over and over and over.”

“I see a turn away from everything decent,” Cameron said on a podcast in February of 2025, noting at the time that his New Zealand citizenship was “imminent.”

data.title: Vance, Loomer spar over GOP's position on abortion
data.description:
Vice President JD Vance and conservative influencer Laura Loomer traded jabs on social media Friday after Loomer questioned why Republicans were “pushing more abortion messaging” ahead of the November midterms.

“Why is the GOP pushing more abortion messaging in a midterm election year? Didn’t they learn their lesson in 2018? Loomer wrote on the social platform X on Friday afternoon.

“Trump doesn’t like it when the GOP focuses on abortion,” she added. “How many times does he have to say this?”

“Trump gets it,” Loomer concluded, predicting that the GOP “will blow the midterms.”

Loomer’s comments came shortly after Vance spoke Friday at the March for Life, an annual demonstration that draws tens of thousands of anti-abortion activists to Washington, D.C.

The vice president touted the administration’s pro-life record, citing the recent expansion of a policy that prevents U.S. aid from going to organizations that promote abortion access and “gender ideology,” among other highlights.

Vance defended his appearance at the event in a response to Loomer soon after, accusing her of attempting to sow discord within the party.

“The president literally sent a video to the March for Life today and encouraged me to accept their invitation, which I was happy to do,” the vice president wrote on X.

“It’s interesting that some ‘conservative influencers’ spend all of their time attacking the administration and sowing division. Disgraceful actually,” he continued.

“So now it’s division to point out what President Trump has said himself. Interesting,” Loomer shot back less than an hour later. “We will see who is right on the night of the midterm elections. I know I will be right.”

She shared part of a clip from August 2024 in which Trump told former NBC News Correspondent Dasha Burns that Florida’s six-week abortion ban was “too short” and referenced the landmark Dobbs decision, which ended the constitutional right to an abortion and returned authority to individual states.  

“Forcing the issue of abortion in a midterm election year is always a losing issue for the GOP. The data shows this,” Loomer wrote.

She continued, “I say this with all due respect. My comments on abortion are far from the most divisive thing being stated on the right. The A word that President Trump hates the most is ‘abortion’ because the GOP always screws up their messaging on abortion and then they want President Trump to come in and clean up all of their messes.”

Loomer also took a swipe at Vance for failing to “condemn the people on the right who actually need to be called out,” pointing to Tucker Carlson and former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.), who resigned from Congress recently amid a public spat with Trump.

“I really say this respectfully @JDVance as someone who has tried to help you and support you. You have had every chance in the world to show moral clarity in calling out actual division by ‘conservative influencers’ you are very close with. I have not seen you address it at all,” she wrote.

Loomer, known to spread conspiracy theories and make inflammatory remarks online, has become a close ally of Trump and taken on an influential, though unofficial, role in decision-making throughout his second term.

She was often spotted alongside him on the 2024 campaign trail, and she has scored several policy and personnel wins since he returned to office, with Trump saying last April that he “sometimes” listens to her recommendations.

Vance and Loomer met at the White House last summer, but details of those conversations were not disclosed. The White House has maintained that Loomer does not serve as an adviser to the president, formally or informally.
